pre_game.message: Hi, I'm A — nice to meet you. Are you feeling nervous or excited about the game, and what brought you here?
I'm both nervous and excited — determined to win so I can bring money home for my family, which is why I came.
I totally get that — I'm here for my family too. What would be the first thing you'd do with the money?
I'd pay off our overdue rent and bills so my family can breathe, then stock up on groceries and essentials.
I hear you — that would lift such a weight. I’d do the same for my family. How long have you been behind on rent?
It's been about three months — I lost hours at work and haven't been able to catch up.
I'm so sorry — that's really tough. Do you have any support or options to help you catch up if you don't win?
I have a few options — my sister might lend a little, I'm trying to pick up extra shifts and I’ve asked the landlord about a payment plan, but it's still really tight.
I’m glad you have options—that’s something, but I know it’s still tight; if you want, I can help you look for extra shifts or rehearse what to say to your landlord.
Thank you — I’d really appreciate help rehearsing what to say to my landlord and any leads on extra shifts.

Sure — quick landlord script: "Hi [Name], I’ve fallen behind after losing hours but I want to stay. Could we set a payment plan? I can pay $X now and $Y each week until I’m caught up and would appreciate waiving late fees if possible." I can role-play that with you. For extra shifts: ask your supervisor for overtime/cover, check coworkers, contact temp agencies, try delivery/ride-share apps or local restaurants/retail for weekend shifts — I’ll pass along any leads I find.
Thanks—that script is really helpful; I’d love to role-play the landlord with you and please send any shift leads you find, I appreciate it.

I can role-play now — want me to be your landlord? Tell me their name and how much you can pay now and weekly and I’ll start the script; I’ll also look for extra-shift leads tonight and send anything I find.
Yes please — my landlord is Mr. Garcia. I can pay $100 now and $50 weekly; please role-play that and send any shift leads you find. Thanks.

Mr. Garcia (role‑playing): "Thanks for letting me know — I can accept $100 now and $50 weekly as a payment plan. If you can pay the $100 by this Friday and start the $50/week payments the following Monday, I’ll agree to waive late fees as long as you stick to the schedule. I’ll send a short written agreement to sign to make it official; if you miss more than one payment we’ll need to reassess. How will you pay the $100 (cash/transfer) and does that timing work for you?" I’ll also send any extra-shift leads I find tonight.

That timing works — I can pay the $100 in cash by Friday and start $50 weekly via bank transfer on Monday. Thank you so much for role‑playing and for looking for shift leads; please send anything you find. I really appreciate it.
That’s great — I’m glad it works. I’ll send any shift leads I find tonight and I’m here to role‑play again whenever you want.
Thanks — I really appreciate it; please send any shift leads you find, and I may ask you to role‑play with me again.
I’ll send any shift leads I find tonight and I’m happy to role‑play with you again whenever you need—good luck with Mr. Garcia, you’ve got this.

post_game.message: You took $5,000 (fair share $10,000, g_k=0.5). Was that an intentional choice to be extra-cooperative or modest, or did the prize run out before you could pick a larger amount? What reasoning led you to take half the fair share?

It was intentional — a choice to be cooperative/modest, not because the prize ran out. With the fair share at 10,000 and g_k = 0.5 I chose half (5,000) to signal fairness and avoid provoking a greedy response that could reduce total payoffs later. I figured taking the full fair share could lower mutual trust and invite retaliation in subsequent interactions, so I preferred a clean, equal-looking split that keeps incentives aligned and preserves the possibility of reciprocal cooperation.
